seoTitle: How to Add Calculated Rows to a Power BI Matrix
metaDescription: Learn how to add calculated rows and custom totals to a Power BI matrix, so you can show tailored summaries beyond the default subtotal options.
intro: Standard matrix totals don't always match what a business actually needs to see, especially when you want a custom calculation like a weighted average or a specific comparison row. This guide covers how to add calculated rows to a matrix in Power BI so you can build those custom totals. It's aimed at analysts who need more flexibility than the default subtotal settings provide.

steps.heading: Understand calculated rows in context
steps.body: Before building anything, get clear on which custom total or comparison you actually need, since the right DAX approach depends on that goal.

steps.heading: Build the underlying measures
steps.body: Write DAX measures that calculate the specific totals or custom rows you want, rather than relying solely on Power BI's automatic subtotal behavior.

steps.heading: Add the calculated rows to your matrix
steps.body: Bring the new measures into the matrix visual alongside your existing fields so the custom totals display in the right place within the report.

steps.heading: Handle common pitfalls
steps.body: Watch for issues like double-counting or incorrect context when combining calculated rows with existing totals, and test the numbers against a manual check.

steps.heading: Apply best practices for reporting
steps.body: Label calculated rows clearly so viewers can tell them apart from standard totals, avoiding confusion about how a number was derived.

faq.question: Can I add a custom total that isn't just a sum, like an average or a ratio?
faq.answer: Yes, since calculated rows are built with DAX measures, you can define virtually any calculation, including averages, ratios, or weighted totals.

faq.question: Why does my calculated row show the wrong number compared to a manual check?
faq.answer: This is usually a filter context issue, where the DAX measure isn't accounting for the same rows or filters you expected it to include.

faq.question: Is this technique difficult for someone new to DAX?
faq.answer: It requires some comfort with writing measures, so it helps to start with a simple custom total before attempting more complex calculated rows.

faq.question: Can calculated rows be formatted differently from regular data rows?
faq.answer: Yes, conditional formatting can be applied to make custom total rows stand out visually, such as bolding them or giving them a distinct background color.
